subject: ap-precalculus
title: Polynomial Functions: Covariation, Rates of Change & Inverse Analysis
stimulus: The function $f$ is a polynomial function with $f(-2) = 0$, $f(0) = -8$, $f(1) = 0$, and $f(3) = 0$. The function $g$ is given by $g(x) = 5 \cdot (1.3)^x$.

parts.prompt:
(i) Based on the given information, write an expression for $f(x)$ as a product of linear factors with a verified leading coefficient. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) The average rates of change of $f$ over the intervals $[-2, -1]$, $[-1, 0]$, and $[0, 1]$ are approximately $-10.67$, $2.67$, and $8$ respectively. Determine whether the average rate of change of $f$ is increasing or decreasing on the interval $[-2, 1]$, and explain what this tells you about the concavity of the graph of $f$ on that interval.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), the zeros $x = -2, 1, 3$ give the factored form $f(x) = a(x+2)(x-1)(x-3)$. Evaluating at $x = 0$: $a(2)(-1)(-3) = 6a = -8$, so $a = -4/3$. Correct answer: $f(x) = -\frac{4}{3}(x+2)(x-1)(x-3)$. Supporting work must show the substitution to find $a$. For part (ii), the sequence $-10.67 \to 2.67 \to 8$ is increasing, so the average rate of change is increasing on $[-2, 1]$, indicating the graph of $f$ is concave up on this interval. Both the conclusion (increasing ARC) and the interpretation (concave up) are required.

scoring_points.description: Correct factored form $f(x) = -\frac{4}{3}(x+2)(x-1)(x-3)$ with supporting work showing $a$ is determined by substituting $f(0) = -8$
common_traps: Forgetting to include the leading coefficient $a$ — the zeros alone do not uniquely determine $f$

scoring_points.description: Correct conclusion that the average rate of change is increasing ($-10.67 \to 2.67 \to 8$) and that the graph of $f$ is therefore concave up on $[-2, 1]$

required_elements: average rate of change is increasing
graph of $f$ is concave up on $[-2, 1]$
alternatives.correct_example: The sequence $-10.67, 2.67, 8$ is increasing, so the average rate of change of $f$ is increasing on $[-2, 1]$. An increasing rate of change means the graph of $f$ is concave up on this interval.

wrong_examples: Stating 'concave down' — incorrect because the ARC is increasing
Stating only 'increasing' without connecting to concavity
common_traps: Confusing an increasing (but negative-to-positive) rate of change with the function itself decreasing — the ARC goes from $-10.67$ to $8$, which is increasing regardless of the sign change

parts.prompt:
(i) The function $h$ is defined by $h(x) = (g \circ f)(x) = g(f(x))$. Find the value of $h(-1)$ as a decimal approximation, or indicate that it is not defined. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) Find all values of $x$ for which $g(x) = 20$, or indicate there are no such values. Express your answer as a decimal approximation.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), $f(-1) = -\frac{4}{3}(1)(-2)(-4) = -\frac{32}{3} \approx -10.667$. Then $h(-1) = g(-32/3) = 5(1.3)^{-32/3} \approx 0.304$ (accept $[0.295, 0.313]$). Supporting work must show the intermediate value $f(-1)$. Decimal accuracy: 3 decimal places. For part (ii), $5(1.3)^x = 20 \Rightarrow (1.3)^x = 4 \Rightarrow x = \frac{\ln 4}{\ln 1.3} \approx 5.284$. First decimal presentation error per question does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Correct decimal approximation $h(-1) \approx 0.304$ with supporting work showing $f(-1) \approx -10.667$ and then $g(f(-1))$
wrong_examples: $h(-1) = 0.30$ (fewer than 3 decimal places — decimal presentation error)
common_traps: Forgetting to first evaluate $f(-1)$ before applying $g$
Reporting fewer than 3 decimal places on the first such error does not earn the point

scoring_points.description: Correct decimal approximation $x \approx 5.284$ with no incorrect values
common_traps: Only one solution exists — the exponential $g$ is strictly increasing so $g(x) = 20$ has exactly one solution

parts.prompt:
The domain of $f$ is restricted to the interval $1 \leq x \leq 3$.

(i) Determine whether $f$ has an inverse function on this restricted domain. Give a reason based on the definition of a function.

(ii) The table below shows selected values of $f$ on the interval $1 \leq x \leq 3$.

| $x$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f(x)$ | $0$ | $4.44$ | $0$ |

Use the table to explain why the graph of $f$ must reach a value above $4$ at some point in the open interval $(1, 3)$, and explain why $f$ cannot have an inverse on the interval $[1, 3]$.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), the answer is NO — $f$ does not have an inverse. The reason must explicitly cite $f(1) = f(3) = 0$ as the counterexample showing two distinct inputs map to the same output. A response that says 'yes' earns 0 for both points. For part (ii), a valid response connects $f(1) = 0$, $f(2) = 4.44$, and $f(3) = 0$ to the non-invertibility: the output $0$ is achieved at two different inputs, so $f$ is not one-to-one. Both points can be earned in part (ii) alone if part (i) has no incorrect response.

scoring_points.description: $f$ does NOT have an inverse on $[1, 3]$; reason must reference the definition of a function and cite $f(1) = f(3) = 0$ as evidence that the same output ($0$) corresponds to two distinct inputs

required_elements: $f(1) = f(3) = 0$ — same output for two inputs
alternatives.correct_example: $f$ does not have an inverse on $[1, 3]$ because $f(1) = f(3) = 0$, so the output $0$ comes from two distinct input values. An inverse function would require each output to correspond to exactly one input.

wrong_examples: Saying '$f$ is not one-to-one' without citing specific function values
common_traps: Citing the horizontal line test or one-to-one language without providing specific function values is insufficient — must show $f(1) = f(3) = 0$

scoring_points.description: Correct explanation using table values: $f(2) = 4.44$ (which exceeds $4$) and $f(1) = f(3) = 0$, so the same output is reached at two inputs — $f$ cannot be one-to-one on $[1, 3]$

required_elements: $f(2) = 4.44 > 4$ means a value above 4 is achieved
$f(1) = f(3) = 0$ means the same output at two inputs
alternatives.correct_example: The table shows $f(2) = 4.44 > 4$, so the graph reaches above $4$ on $(1, 3)$. Since $f(1) = 0$ and $f(3) = 0$, the output value $0$ is achieved at two different inputs, so $f$ cannot have an inverse on $[1, 3]$.

wrong_examples: Explaining only the value above $4$ without addressing the non-invertibility
common_traps: Must use specific values from the table — a general statement about increasing then decreasing is insufficient without citing $f(1) = f(3) = 0$
